# Copilot Instructions for MiscBenchmarks

## Project Overview

A collection of ~300+ standalone BenchmarkDotNet micro-benchmark projects comparing different .NET implementation approaches. Each benchmark is an independent executable in its own folder — there is no shared solution file or common base class.

## Build & Run

```powershell
# Build all projects (from repo root)
dotnet build dirs.proj

# Build a single benchmark
cd ArraySum
dotnet build

# Run a single benchmark (must use Release)
cd ArraySum
dotnet run -c Release

# Debug a single benchmark (runs methods directly, no BDN harness)
cd ArraySum
dotnet run

# Run all benchmarks (long-running)
.\run_all_benchmarks.ps1
.\run_all_benchmarks.ps1 -DryRun    # preview what would run

# Monitor progress of run_all_benchmarks.ps1
.\show_benchmark_progress.ps1

# Update a benchmark's README with results (from the benchmark folder)
..\update_results.ps1
```

There are no unit tests or linters in this repository.

## Architecture

- **`dirs.proj`** — MSBuild Traversal SDK file that references all benchmark projects; serves as the repo-wide build entry point.
- **`Directory.Build.props`** — Defines `$(BenchmarkDotNetVersion)` (currently `0.15.2`) used by most projects.
- **`tools\UpdateResults`** — Internal tool that reads BenchmarkDotNet artifact output (`BenchmarkDotNet.Artifacts\results\*-report-github.md`) and writes it into each benchmark's `README.md`.
- **`run_all_benchmarks.ps1`** — Orchestrates running every benchmark, tracks progress in `benchmark_progress.json`, and auto-updates READMEs.
- **Framework scripts** — `update_to_multi_framework.ps1` and `update_to_net10_only.ps1` toggle projects between `net9.0;net10.0` multi-targeting and `net10.0`-only.

## Creating a New Benchmark

Use the BenchmarkDotNet template:

```powershell
dotnet new benchmark -n MyBenchmarkName
cd MyBenchmarkName
```

Then add the new project to `dirs.proj`.

### Standard Project Layout

```
MyBenchmark/
├── Benchmark.cs          # Benchmark class
├── Program.cs            # Entry point with Release/Debug split
├── MyBenchmark.csproj    # Targets net10.0
└── README.md             # Results (auto-generated by update_results.ps1)
```

### Program.cs Pattern

Every project follows this dual-mode pattern:

```csharp
#if RELEASE
    BenchmarkRunner.Run<Benchmark>();
#else
    var b = new Benchmark();
    b.Size = 100;
    b.GlobalSetup();
    var result = b.SomeMethod();
    Console.WriteLine(result);
#endif
```

Debug mode instantiates the benchmark directly for quick validation. Release mode runs the full BenchmarkDotNet harness.

### Benchmark Class Conventions

- Class is typically named `Benchmark` in namespace `Test`.
- Apply `[MemoryDiagnoser]` to measure allocations.
- Apply `[SimpleJob(RuntimeMoniker.Net10_0)]` for the target runtime.
- Use `[Params(...)]` with two sizes (one small, one large) for parameterized input.
- Use `[GlobalSetup]` for data initialization.
- Mark one method `[Benchmark(Baseline = true)]`; other methods are `[Benchmark]`.
- Return results from benchmark methods to prevent dead code elimination.
- Do not add `MethodImplOptions.NoInlining` unless truly necessary.
- Keep the number of benchmark method variations focused and distinct.

### .csproj Pattern

```xml
<Project Sdk="Microsoft.NET.Sdk">
  <PropertyGroup>
    <OutputType>Exe</OutputType>
    <TargetFramework>net10.0</TargetFramework>
  </PropertyGroup>
  <ItemGroup>
    <PackageReference Include="BenchmarkDotNet" Version="$(BenchmarkDotNetVersion)" />
  </ItemGroup>
</Project>
```

Use `$(BenchmarkDotNetVersion)` from `Directory.Build.props` rather than hardcoding the version.
